title: Church of Satan
description: Church of Satan is a book by Blanche Barton. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime.
descriptionParagraphs: Church of Satan is a book by Blanche Barton. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime.

What makes it interesting is that interpretation, devotion, satire, or doctrinal conflict becomes a matter of state administration. As a book, it can be read not only for subject matter but for the way form, tone, and circulation make a text feel dangerous, intimate, or politically usable to anxious officials.

It also matters as part of a wider censorship history in Michigan. The present page is a dossier starter built from source-tracked ban records; the surviving note currently says: The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: Threat to. More publication history, translations, and close reading can be added later.

overview.englishDescription: Church of Satan is a book by Blanche Barton. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime.
banningParagraphs: Church of Satan entered censorship debates as a book associated with doctrine, public controversy, and religion. In the current dossier, the main state objections cluster around instructional harm, public order, religious control, and religious offense.

The earliest event currently captured here is 2000-12-01 in Michigan, where Michigan corrections agencies and prison mailrooms excluded from prison circulation. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: Threat to. The Michigan prison-ban record treats the book as excluded reading inside state custody, which shows how prison and mailroom censorship function as a government reading regime. The exported reason says: Threat to order/security institution

counterReadings.title: Assassins of the Mind
counterReadings.author: Christopher Hitchens
counterReadings.note: Frames the Rushdie affair as a test of free speech against violent religious intimidation.

counterReadings.title: From Fatwa to Jihad
counterReadings.author: Kenan Malik
counterReadings.note: Tracks how conflicts over blasphemy, race, and offense evolved after the Rushdie controversy.

counterReadings.title: 100 Banned Books: Censorship Histories of World Literature
counterReadings.author: Nicholas J. Karolides, Margaret Bald, and Dawn B. Sova
counterReadings.note: A compact reference on how censorship systems moved across states, churches, and courts.

counterReadings.title: Banned Books: 387 B.C. to 1978 A.D.
counterReadings.author: Anne Lyon Haight
counterReadings.note: Useful for comparing older obscenity, heresy, and political bans with modern free-speech disputes.
